content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

July 18, 1990


Ann M. Peifer
Office of Paul Carpenter
Member, State Board of Equalization
4040 Paramount Blvd., Suite 103
Lakewood, CA 90712

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-90-428

Dear Ms. Peifer:

You are seeking advice on behalf of Paul Carpenter regarding
his duties and responsibilities as a member of the Board of Equal-
ization under the contributions limitations provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ The following advice is based
upon the facts provided in your letter of June 22, 1990, and in a
telephone conversation on June 27, 1990, and is provided to you as
Mr. Carpenter's authorized representative. (Regulation 18329, copy
enclosed.) It is limited to the provisions of the Act and does
not addressee other provisions of state or federal law which might
also be applicable to your query.

QUESTION

Mr. Carpenter is treasurer of a federal campaign committee.
He wishes to terminate the committee and deposit the committee's
remaining funds into his reelection committee account. Is this
permitted under the Act?

CONCLUSION

Mr. Carpenter may deposit funds from the federal committee
into his reelection committee account provided the total funds do
not exceed the Act's applicable contribution limits.


_____________________________

¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Di-
vision 6 of the California Code of Regulations.


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916) 322-5660

File No. A-90-428
Page 2

FACTS

     Paul Carpenter, a member of the Board of Equalization, is
treasurer of a federal political committee, "Friends of Paul Car-
penter" ("federal committee"). The federal committee was formed
for his candidacy for the United States Senate in 1982 and has
remained in existence after he was unsuccessful in the race.
Mr. Carpenter wishes to terminate the federal committee and
deposit its existing assets, approximately $472.00, into his re-
election committee campaign account, the "Carpenter Campaign Com-
mittee" ("campaign committee").

ANALYSIS

     Mr. Carpenter, as an elected member of the Board of Equaliza-
tion, is a "candidate" for purposes of the Act. (Sections 82007,
82203.) The Act permits a candidate to accept a contribution from
a person up to $1,000, from a political committee up to $2,500,
and from a broad based political committee or political party up
to $5,000, per fiscal year. (Sections 85301(a), 85303(a),
85303(b).)²

     The transfer of funds from a federal campaign committee to a
state campaign committee is not prohibited by the Act. (Levin
Advice Letter, No. I-89-687, copy enclosed.) A federal candi-
date's committee is considered a "person" and not a "committee"
under the Act and for purposes of the Act's contribution limits.
(Stark Advice Letter, No. A-89-470, copy enclosed.)

     Therefore, Mr. Carpenter's federal committee can contribute
up to $1,000 per fiscal year to his campaign committee. Since the
amount remaining in the federal committee's account is under this
threshold, the funds may be contributed to the campaign committee.


2    Section 85304 also prohibits a candidate or his or her con-
trolled committee from transferring funds to another candidate or
controlled committee, although enforcement of this section has
been enjoined by the federal court in Service Employees Interna-
tional Union, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (U.S.
District Court, Eastern District of California, Case No. CIVs 89-
0433 LKK-JFM) to the extent that the section would prohibit a
candidate from transferring campaign funds among his or her own
controlled committees.

File No. A-90-428
Page 3


     I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you
requested. If you have any further questions regarding this mat-
ter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
     By: Jonathan S. Rothman
         Counsel, Legal Division

KED:JSR:plh

Enclosures

Mr. Carpenter may deposit funds from the federal committee into his reelection committee account provided the total funds do not exceed the Act's applicable contribution limits.

, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

The federal committee was formed for his candidacy for the United States Senate in 1982 and has remained in existence after he was unsuccessful in the race. Mr. Carpenter wishes to terminate the federal committee and deposit its existing assets, approximately $472.00, into his re-election committee campaign account, the "Carpenter Campaign Committee" ("campaign committee").
sections.analysis:
Mr. Carpenter, as an elected member of the Board of Equalization, is a "candidate" for purposes of the Act. (Sections 82007, 82023.) The Act permits a candidate to accept a contribution from a person up to $1,000, from a political committee up to $2,500, and from a broad based political committee or political party up to $5,000, per fiscal year. (Sections 85301(a), 85303(a), 85303(b).)2

The transfer of funds from a federal campaign committee to a state campaign committee is not prohibited by the Act. (Levin Advice Letter, No. I-89-687, copy enclosed.) A federal candidate's committee is considered a "person" and not a "committee" under the Act and for purposes of the Act's contribution limits. (Stark Advice Letter, No. A-89-470, copy enclosed.)

Therefore, Mr. Carpenter's federal committee can contribute up to $1,000 per fiscal year to his campaign committee. Since the amount remaining in the federal committee's account is under this threshold, the funds may be contributed to the campaign committee.

2 Section 85304 also prohibits a candidate or his or her controlled committee from transferring funds to another candidate or controlled committee, although enforcement of this section has been enjoined by the federal court in Service Employees International Union, et al. v. Fair Political Practices Commission (U.S. District Court, Eastern District of California, Case NO. CIVS 89-0433 LKK-JFM) to the extent that the section would prohibit a candidate from transferring campaign funds among his or her own controlled committees.

I trust this letter has provided you with the guidance you requested. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.
